question.text: Which of the following best describes Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs)?

answer.text: Mutual funds that can only be bought during NFO
Mutual funds that track an index and are traded on stock exchanges like stocks throughout the day
Closed-ended funds that are mandatorily listed

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ETFs are mutual funds that usually track an index (equity or commodity like gold), are listed and traded on stock exchanges throughout the day like stocks, and have real-time prices. Unlike regular open-ended funds which have a single daily NAV, ETFs have multiple intraday prices. **The Trap:** ETFs are not limited to NFO purchase — after listing, they are bought/sold on exchanges through brokers. **Cross-Exam Context:** ETF structure and features are tested in V-A, XII, and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ETFs offer lower expense ratios than actively managed funds but require a demat account and broker — assess whether the client has the necessary infrastructure before recommending.
